mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar I‑Pace EV400 drive unit replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned EV400 drive unit typically costs £5,000–£8,000 supply only, with the rear motor at the higher end. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500 depending on the specialist. A documented case reported a £26,000 USD motor replacement cost (approximately £20,000). Independent specialists offer savings up to £2,800 versus main dealer pricing. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your I‑Pace.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar I‑Pace EV400 drive unit reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The I‑Pace has a mixed reputation. Owners have reported electric motor bearing wear, inverter capacitor degradation, 12V battery failures, steering rack failures, and windscreen leaks. Jaguar dealerships are not authorised to work on the electric motor internally — the whole motor must be removed and sent to Jaguar HQ. Independent specialists offer reconditioned units with upgraded bearings and capacitors. A well‑maintained I‑Pace with an active battery warranty can be a rewarding EV.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar I‑Pace EV400?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issues are electric motor bearing wear (steering vibrations at speed), inverter capacitor degradation (power reduction warnings), 12V battery failure (electrical gremlins), steering rack failures, and windscreen leaks. Later cars have seen improvements, but early examples (2018–2020) are more prone to issues.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an I‑Pace EV400 drive unit, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the vehicle value and condition. Against a well‑kept I‑Pace worth £20,000–£35,000, a £6,000–£8,000 drive unit replacement is 20–30% of the car's value — often worth it for a cherished example. Against a tired early car with significant body or battery issues, the same replacement may exceed our 60% Jaguar threshold — get a scrap valuation first. Cars with active battery warranty command a premium.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar I‑Pace EV400 powertrain code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The powertrain code is EV400, found on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation. It's the dual‑motor all‑wheel‑drive system fitted to all I‑Pace models from 2018‑present. If your I‑Pace has a single motor, it's a different variant.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the EV400 electric motor bearings, or does the motor need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The bearings can be replaced with upgraded ceramic hybrid bearings by specialist EV workshops. Jaguar dealerships are not authorised to work on the motor internally and will only offer complete motor replacement. A bearing replacement costs £2,500–£4,500 — far cheaper than a £5,000–£12,000+ motor replacement. At the first sign of steering vibrations or motor noise, book a specialist inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Jaguar I‑Pace EV400 Drive Unit Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current I‑Pace EV400 drive unit replacement costs. The rear motor is generally more expensive to replace than the front due to access complexity. See the Era Map above for generation‑specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an I‑Pace EV400 drive unit typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Main dealer labour rates can reach £180–£240 per hour.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: I‑Pace EV400 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — upgraded ceramic bearings available
Straightforward replacement — wear and tear item
Only if caught early; often more cost‑effective to replace with reconditioned unit
Viable repair — known issue on earlier cars
Covered under 8‑year / 100,000‑mile warranty if still active

buyingChecks.title: Buying This I‑Pace EV400? Check These First
buyIf: * Battery warranty still active (8 years / 100,000 miles)
* No steering vibrations at speed (motor bearing health)
* No power reduction warnings (inverter capacitor health)
* Full service history with documented 12V battery checks
* Still under Jaguar Approved Used warranty or extended warranty
avoidIf: * No remaining battery warranty — replacement costs are significant
* Steering vibrations or motor noises — bearing replacement is expensive
* Power reduction warning lights — inverter capacitor issues may be developing
* No service history — EVs still need regular inspections
* Any history of motor or inverter failure — walk away

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The I‑Pace EV400 is Jaguar’s first all‑electric vehicle — a 400 PS dual‑motor SUV that delivers impressive performance and a premium driving experience. However, it has a reputation for reliability concerns. Owners have reported electric motor bearing wear, inverter capacitor degradation, 12V battery failures, steering rack failures, and windscreen leaks. A documented case reported a £26,000 USD motor replacement cost for a car with 77,000 km. Jaguar dealerships are not authorised to work on the electric motor — replacement requires removing the motor, sending it to Jaguar HQ, and installing a new unit. Independent specialists like Engine Finders offer reconditioned units with upgraded bearings and capacitors at significant savings versus main dealer pricing. With typical I‑Pace values ranging from £16,000 to £40,000+, a drive unit replacement is a major financial commitment — a car with an active battery warranty and a full service history is the safest choice.

cta.label: → Compare I‑Pace EV400 drive unit prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar I‑Pace EV400 (or before you commit to a drive unit replacement), check these:
items: * Steering Vibrations — Test drive at motorway speeds; any vibration may indicate motor bearing wear — see the map above
* Power Reduction Warnings — Check for any dashboard warnings; inverter capacitor issues may be developing
* Battery Warranty — Confirm 8‑year / 100,000‑mile battery warranty is still active and transferable
* Service History — Including 12V battery checks and software updates (EVs still need regular maintenance)
* 12V Battery — Check for recent replacement; failing 12V batteries cause electrical gremlins
* Windscreen Seals — Check for leaks; a known issue on earlier cars
* Steering Rack — Test for any play or knocking; steering rack failures have been reported
* Charging System — Test both AC and DC charging; any errors may indicate charging system faults
* MOT History — Check for any advisory notes related to suspension or electrical systems
